lesson.title: ARCH & GARCH Models
lesson.body: Financial return series often exhibit volatility clustering: large shocks are followed by large shocks. ARCH(q) models this with conditional variance σ²_t = α₀ + Σαᵢε²_{t-i}. GARCH(1,1) adds a lagged variance term: σ²_t = ω + αε²_{t-1} + βσ²_{t-1}. Stationarity of GARCH(1,1) requires α + β < 1. The model captures 'fat tails' and volatility clustering seen in financial data.

rooms.question: Volatility clustering in financial time series means:
answers: Periods of high volatility tend to be followed by high volatility
Volatility only occurs at market open
rooms.explanation: Volatility clustering: large price changes tend to cluster together. This violates i.i.d. assumptions and motivates GARCH-type models.

rooms.question: In GARCH(1,1): σ²_t = ω + αε²_{t-1} + βσ²_{t-1}, the parameter β represents:
answers: Persistence of volatility from the previous period's variance
rooms.explanation: β is the 'volatility persistence' parameter — how much of yesterday's conditional variance carries forward. High β means slow volatility decay.

rooms.question: The unconditional variance of a stationary GARCH(1,1) is:
rooms.explanation: Taking unconditional expectation of GARCH(1,1): E[σ²_t] = ω + (α+β)E[σ²_t], solving: σ² = ω/(1−α−β).

rooms.question: Order the steps to fit a GARCH(1,1) model to financial returns:
items: Test for ARCH effects (Engle's LM test on squared residuals)
Check standardized residuals for remaining patterns
rooms.explanation: Test for ARCH effects first (check if GARCH is needed), specify model, MLE estimation, then diagnostics.

rooms.question: GARCH models are typically applied to:
answers: Returns (first differences of log prices), not price levels
rooms.explanation: GARCH models the conditional variance of returns r_t = log(P_t/P_{t-1}). Price levels are non-stationary; returns are approximately stationary.

boss.name: The Volatility Demon
boss.taunt: Calm periods breed complacency. I strike when least expected, then cluster.

questions.question: Engle's ARCH-LM test regresses squared residuals ε²_t on:
answers: Their own lagged values ε²_{t-1}, ..., ε²_{t-q}
questions.explanation: ARCH-LM test: regress ε²_t on ε²_{t-1},...,ε²_{t-q}. Under H₀ (no ARCH), R² ≈ 0. nR² ~ χ²(q) under H₀.

questions.question: Integrated GARCH (IGARCH) occurs when α + β = 1, implying:
answers: The process is stationary with unit variance
questions.explanation: IGARCH (α+β=1) means unconditional variance is infinite and shocks never die out. Often seen in high-frequency financial data.

questions.question: EGARCH (Exponential GARCH) extends GARCH to allow for the 'leverage effect', which means:
answers: Negative returns increase volatility more than positive returns of the same size
Variance is always positive by construction
questions.explanation: The leverage effect: stock price drops → higher leverage → increased volatility. Symmetric GARCH misses this. EGARCH models log σ²_t allowing asymmetry.
